posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked:
Ok all the firm talk on d’Yquem winning the tasting contest has me interested in buying a bottle. Ive only had it a few times at restaurants or tastings.

I’m am decidedly not a sweet tooth, but do feel as the benchmark it’s probably something we should do with our own bottle at home where it can be more leisurely savored.

What’s drinking well out of 375 right now because what am I going to do with 750ml!

Retail availability is a must and if you know the price range I’d appreciate that as well, since it’s not an area I’ve ventured

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
88-90 are spectacular right now from 375. They should be pretty widely available at places like Benchmark. They actually have 3 375s of the 88 for $240, which is quite a good price.

01 may ultimately be the best Yquem of all time, closely followed by 09, but both are quite tight right now.

I have recent notes on 88-90 on my instagram.

Are you calling and asking about or for pics of color when buying these?

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
Robert_M_yers:

Thanks gentlemen. I will take a look.

What might the oldest nice vintage be that’s still “classic” and not on the downside?

Are you calling and asking about or for pics of color when buying these?

Yquem lasts a long time, the 53 wasn’t on the downside. The price and availability are going to start to escalate very quickly once you start getting older than the mid 80s.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: 88-90 are excellent vintages. Personally, I find the 90 too sweet, so would steer you to 88 or 89 instead. If you can find it, 1975 might be the sweet spot for great vintage, with enough age to really be in its prime.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
Alan_Rath:

88-90 are excellent vintages. Personally, I find the 90 too sweet, so would steer you to 88 or 89 instead. If you can find it, 1975 might be the sweet spot for great vintage, with enough age to really be in its prime.

I’m not sure how easy it would be to get the 75 in 375 and how good it would be in that format; there are lots of 88-90 though and they’ve been drinking well recently in 375z

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: Off vintages of Yquem are usually pretty damn good as well, don’t run away from a good deal on almost any vintage.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
Alan_Rath:

Off vintages of Yquem are usually pretty damn good as well, don’t run away from a good deal on almost any vintage.

Yeah that’s definitely true… however as I said earlier benchmark has the 88 in 375 for 240; off vintages like 97 are the same price. I’ve been backfilling a ton of Yquem lately, that’s a very good price.

posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked: Does extended age tend to level the playing field between vintages?

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
Robert_M_yers:

Does extended age tend to level the playing field between vintages?

No. The better vintages are always better.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: I don’t think I have enough experience to answer that.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
MChang:
Robert_M_yers:

Does extended age tend to level the playing field between vintages?

No. The better vintages are always better.

It kind of depends on individual taste. Sometimes the “better” vintages are sweeter, or almost just “too much”. I’ve done comparative tastings where I enjoyed a lesser vintage over a celebrated vintage.

posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked:
Well that makes sense but does it trend that way with sweet wines or does the divide become more prominent?

I’m imagining there’s probably a point when the lesser wine gets too tired…

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: I think the better vintages always seem like they have more potential, even when young. If you’re talking about aged wines the better vintages are DEFINITELY better. The top vintages from the 20s taste like they’re from the 50s.

posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked: Yes, I’m imagining I less ripe vintage would be a better choice for us.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: you should get the 88. It’s a perfect age for the 375, and less sweet than the 90 (and perhaps the 89). It’s among the best vintages yquem has made in the last 30 years (along with 89, 90, 01, 09).

posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked:
Appreciate the further distinctions…

Just out of curiosity.
What would be the vintage(s) that is just unexpectedly too young to be past or aging too fast? With the legendary ageability are there vintages that have not done as expected?

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
Robert_M_yers:

Appreciate the further distinctions…

Just out of curiosity.
What would be the vintage(s) that is just unexpectedly too young to be past or aging too fast? With the legendary ageability are there vintages that have not done as expected?

Not for me, but I haven’t aged anything younger than 99; I’ve had probably 500 bottles of Yquem from 70 different vintages from the 1800s onwards though and it’s always good. I can’t remember a pour of Yquem that wasn’t good. It’s the most consistent wine, period.

posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked: That’s is a pretty amazing statistic for wine. certainly explains your insistence to take one to a contest!

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: grandvinwinemerchants is apparently closing and has a closeout sale with some deals on yquem like 2011 for 319 also; I don’t like 2011 as much as the 88 but that’s a full bottle at the best price in the US.

posts.name: Michael Hodgins
posts.cooked: You convinced me! Just bought a 375 of 1988—appreciate your insights

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: excited to see what you think!

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: Vinsrare also has excellent pricing on yquem atm; they have 750s of 99 and 03 for $300 and 375s of 88 for 239 and 90 for 249. Also 96 for 175.
Where the hell is Ashish when you need him?! @AAgrawal

posts.name: David Glasser
posts.cooked: Another vote for ‘88 or ‘89. Both are stunning now.

posts.name: Ashish
posts.cooked:
I missed this! I’ve been spending all my free time planning an all-sauternes dinner for next week. =)

My vote is also for the '88 Yquem. I tell anyone who asks that it’s by far my favorite Yquem for drinking now. At the price it goes for, I still think it’s a value. '75 and '76 are great too, but I rarely see half bottles and they are a bit more pricey.

posts.name: Ashish
posts.cooked: I would also say that no, I haven’t had any vintages which have advanced too fast or are past it. I’m actually a big fan of some vintages like 2003 that you think would be weaker or have flaws. I suppose if one had to pick the weakest vintage in the past few decades, a contender would be '99. It’s decent, but not great Yquem.

posts.name: Yule Kim
posts.cooked: '88 d’Yquem was drinking pretty well today. Go for it.

posts.name: Tom Reddick
posts.cooked:
You cannot go wrong with 1988 or 1990- the 1989 is great too but a bit excessive for my tastes.

I have not had one lately, but there was talk of the 1975 and I have some halves left and I would be shocked if they are over the hill.

As far as the great vintages- I think that depends to some degree on what you want. There are certainly lesser examples, but I find most vintages of Yquem to be worthy of attention and my choice depends a lot on what is being served alongside for food (note that in my family we love Yquem and there is even some precedent for having it with a main course- so we do not just trot it out for dessert.)

And on that note- others worth trying now,

1986 is in a perfect place and for me an archetypal example. One for dessert. I prefer it to the 88-90 trio, though if I were objectively applying a 100 point scale I do not think it would register a higher number.

1997 is more rounded and versatile and has been really out there and terrific since release. Good for starter, the right entree or a lighter/savory dessert.

2009 is mindblowing and has years of development left, but really something to experience as often as you can. Another one mainly for dessert- about as decadent as it gets.

2015 is another one to enjoy all along the way I think. More tension here but still decadent. Dessert or with a course of foie gras. 30 years from now, I think comparing the 09 to the 15 will be a fascinating exercise.

2010 is a rare example that is going to need a long time to fully show itself- but I think it an important vintage and right now while it is somewhat shut down it makes for a good entree companion. And that will likely continue FWIW- it an Yquem of exceptional muscle but I am not sure it will ever have the opulence of the 2009. So while not on my short list of examples to open showing their best, a good one to look in on if you find that you want to try a few others in the near term for comparisons.

2017 is also very good- more in the vein of 1997 and probably the best very young Yquem to try if you find that it is younger vintages that are more readily available to you.

The 75 is a bit more structured but the 86 was one of the best bottles from the case of 24 I bought. I also agree that the 88 is one of the best vintages, with 75 and 01.

posts.name: Richard Albert
posts.cooked: Thanks for the update on the 75 375 and 750 and am still in no hurry to open them.

posts.name: Neil Thomas
posts.cooked: Totally agree with you - bought them all on release. Now I’m into the ‘01 from 375mls. as my “go to”. My favourite when young was always the ‘88.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
NeilT:

Totally agree with you - bought them all on release. Now I’m into the ‘01 from 375mls. as my “go to”. My favourite when young was always the ‘88.

I think the 01 is way too young to open right now; we had it last year and it was very tight and monolithic.

posts.name: Neil Thomas
posts.cooked: You should have tasted it 6 yrs ago!

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
NeilT:

You should have tasted it 6 yrs ago!

I have tasted it probably 30 times since it was released because people keep opening them. Earlier it was sort of fun to drink in a hedonistic sort of way, now it’s just a waste. I have cases of this wine in sizes up to 6L, I think it’ll be the best wine they’ve ever made at the estate.

posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked:
MChang:

up to 6L

A Wedding?

For current drinking of recent best vintages then is 09>01?

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
Robert_M_yers:
MChang:

up to 6L

I’m curious where you think you might eventually open that?

A Wedding?

For current drinking of recent best vintages then is 09>01?

Lots of occasions; we don’t have a problem getting through Sauternes. At many of our wine dinners we open 3 750s.

I wouldn’t drink either of those vintages right now. Not ready. If you’re going to drink something young drink something like 17, 16, 11, 13 etc. 88 is the best vintage for you to get, though.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
Alan_Rath:

Sometimes the “better” vintages are sweeter, or almost just “too much”.

I don’t find them sweeter, but much better acid balance. In fact the sweetest d’Yquem I’ve had was the 03 that had no acid to balance the ripeness. It was just sweet and flat. So I would say it’s quite the opposite for me.

posts.name: Abbie_Sal0v0n
posts.cooked:
I’ve got '70, '98 and '04 (and '16), if you want to compare.
My '70 (2 bottles) are 750’s so would need more than a few people.

posts.name: David Glasser
posts.cooked:
Andrew_K:
Alan_Rath:

Sometimes the “better” vintages are sweeter, or almost just “too much”.

I don’t find them sweeter, but much better acid balance. In fact the sweetest d’Yquem I’ve had was the 03 that had no acid to balance the ripeness. It was just sweet and flat. So I would say it’s quite the opposite for me.

I agree with Andrew here. One borderline exception is the 1990. A great vintage that sometimes shows as you describe it Alan. Not always, but sometimes.

Some (all?) of that perception can be palate preference. There are those who feel all of Barsac/Sauternes, Yquem included, is too sweet (I’m not one of them), and that German Riesling is where it’s at (no argument from me: BAs and TBAs can be terrific).
